title: DRAIN THE SWAMP Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
plain_summary: This law forces government agencies to move at least 30 percent of their Washington DC workers to offices in other states within one year. Agencies must also get rid of at least 30 percent of their office space in Washington DC within two years. Workers who get moved cannot work from home full time anymore, and they will be paid based on their new location. Some important agencies like the Department of Defense and CIA do not have to follow these rules. The goal is to spread government jobs around the country instead of keeping them all in Washington DC.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
subjects: Government employee pay, benefits, personnel management
plain_keywords: government jobs moving out of washington

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Decentralizing and Reorganizing Agency Infrastructure Nation-wide To Harness Efficient Services, Workforce Administration, and Management Practices Act. It can also be called the DRAIN THE SWAMP Act for short.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following. Budget justification materials means the documents that government agencies send to Congress to explain why they need money, as defined in an existing federal law. Employee means a federal government worker as defined in existing law, but does not include workers who would still have to work during a government shutdown because their jobs defend America from immediate threats. This exception also does not apply to people working in the President's office or in national security jobs at the Department of Defense, Department of Energy, Department of Homeland Security, Office of the Director of National Intelligence, or Central Intelligence Agency. Executive agency means any government department or office that is part of the executive branch. Headquarters employee means a federal worker whose main workplace is at the main office of their agency, or someone who works from home full time but gets paid based on Washington area wages. Headquarters means the main building that serves as the management and administrative center of a government agency. Pay locality means the geographic area used to determine how much federal workers get paid in different parts of the country. Rural means any area that the Census Bureau does not call urban. Telework means working from home or another location away from the office. Telework on a full-time basis means a worker is allowed to work from home for one hundred percent of their work days each pay period. Washington metropolitan area means the geographic region where workers get paid at Washington area rates. Washington metropolitan area rate of pay means the pay rate for the area designated as Washington-Baltimore-Arlington, DC-MD-VA-WV-PA.

sections.title: Section 3 Relocation of Employees
sections.text: Within one year after this law takes effect, the head of each government agency must move at least thirty percent of their headquarters workers to offices outside the Washington area. These new work locations must be spread throughout different regions where the agency operates. For each worker who gets moved, the agency must make sure that worker gets paid based on the local pay rate for their new location, not Washington area wages. These moved workers cannot work from home full time. When deciding where to move workers, agency heads must promote geographic diversity and consider rural areas. They must also make sure there are enough workers in each region to provide good in-person customer service. All headquarters workers are eligible to be moved, except for workers with disabilities who have been given permission to work from home full time as a reasonable accommodation under the Americans with Disabilities Act. These disability exception workers still count toward the total number of headquarters employees when calculating the thirty percent requirement. Agency heads must notify workers who are eligible to be moved by the day before they submit their report to Congress. Within one hundred eighty days after this law takes effect, each agency head must send a report to the congressional committees that oversee their agency. The report must include how many headquarters workers they have, how many are eligible to be moved, how many will actually be moved, how many have disability exceptions, and their plan for making these changes. Between sixty and ninety days after submitting this report, agency heads must notify workers whose jobs are being moved. The notification must tell workers that ninety days later, their workplace will change, their pay will be based on the new location, and they cannot work from home full time. The notice must also tell them where their new workplace will be. For headquarters workers who currently work from home full time but are not being moved to a new location, they will lose the ability to work from home full time one hundred eighty days after their agency submits its report to Congress. This applies to full-time remote workers who do not have disability exceptions.

sections.title: Section 4 Reduction in Headquarters Office Space
sections.text: Within sixty days after this law takes effect, the Director of the Office of Management and Budget must issue a memo directing that government agencies reduce their headquarters office space by at least thirty percent. This includes both buildings the government owns and buildings it rents. When deciding which properties to sell or stop renting, the director should prioritize getting rid of entire buildings and combining agency headquarters into as few locations as possible. If an agency head is told to reduce office space under this memo, they must start reducing space within one hundred eighty days after this law takes effect. They must finish reducing their office space within two years after this law takes effect.

sections.title: Section 5 Information Included in Budget Justification Materials Provided to Congress
sections.text: Each agency head must include specific information in their budget requests to Congress starting with the first budget request after this law takes effect and in every budget request after that. They must report how many headquarters employees they have. They must report how many employees work at field offices, district offices, or regional offices. They must report how many employees work from home full time. They must also report how many employees work from home full time as a disability accommodation under the Americans with Disabilities Act.

sections.title: Section 6 No Relocation Incentives
sections.text: If this law causes a worker's official workplace to change from their home to their agency's headquarters, that worker cannot be paid any relocation incentive money, regardless of what any other law says.

sections.title: Section 7 Severability
sections.text: If any part of this law is found to be unconstitutional, the rest of the law will still be in effect and will still apply to other people and situations.

sections.title: Section 8 Supersession
sections.text: This law overrides any other law and any union contract or labor agreement that conflicts with it.

sections.title: Section 9 No Private Cause of Action
sections.text: Nothing in this law gives any person the right to file a lawsuit to challenge any selection, change, decision, or action taken under this law.
